what_it_does: Records a design finding that channel membership currently controls message delivery rather than stored-data access, and argues for extending the member registry to gate read/run access as graph edges.
observations: Membership today gates message delivery, not data access.
There is no existing mechanism that restricts read access by channel membership.
The default 'every member can do everything' matches the current absence of gating.
Read/run permissions are proposed as a third registry attribute alongside awake/listening and alive/busy.
'Member X can read this dragnet' is modeled as an edge from a dragnet node to a member node in the graph.
standouts: The central claim that membership gates delivery, not access.
The argument that default-open is the current reality, not a chosen policy.
The proposal to fold access control into the same graph-edge mechanism used for scope.
conventions: Uses noticeboard item YAML frontmatter with id, address, type, source, state, title, links, and timestamps.
Body is Markdown with a heading and bullet list.
Treats the noticeboard item as both a factual finding and an architectural recommendation.
concerns: Assumes the absence of an access-control mechanism proves intentional default-open behavior.
Extends a delivery/posture registry to authorization without examining whether its semantics fit access control.
Does not specify how read/run permissions would be enforced at the storage or API layer.
notes: A design note arguing that channel membership currently controls live message delivery, not stored data visibility, and proposing read/run gating as new registry attributes mapped to graph edges.
questions: Which storage or query layer currently exposes dragnet findings to non-members?
What is the exact schema of the member registry beyond posture and liveness?
How would read versus run permissions be represented and enforced in code?
Does 'delivery' include historical message retrieval or only live push?
purpose_vs_actual: The stated purpose is to present the most important finding about access/feeding points; the file does that and extends it into an architectural proposal, so purpose and actual align.
apparent_themes: Unified graph model for scope and authorization.
Reusing the member registry for access control.
intention_signals: It is trying to reframe access control not as a separate permission system but as a natural extension of the existing member registry and graph-edge model, making the proposed restriction mechanism feel native.
novelty: It explicitly separates message delivery semantics from data access semantics and uses that gap to justify a new application of the member registry.
contribution: It contributes the insight that membership currently governs delivery rather than data access, plus a proposal to model read/run authorization as registry attributes and graph edges, which a fused system could keep as one design option for access control.
summary_for_embedding: A noticeboard item stating that channel membership currently gates message delivery rather than data access, and proposing to extend the member registry with read/run permissions represented as graph edges.
intention_for_embedding: To clarify the actual semantics of channel membership and argue for adding member-based read/run gating within the existing registry and graph model.
